title: Bahrain Reactions: Williams Ecstatic, Keisner Fumes, and Ferrari Receive a Wake‑Up Call
meta: Published: 13 April 2026 | By Olly Bradshaw, Paddock Insider | F1CC News
caption: Joshua Nathanial’s breakthrough victory was the feel‑good story of Bahrain — but elsewhere, tempers frayed.
body:
Joshua Nathanial won the Bahrain Grand Prix. Williams finished second and third. Ferrari, for the first time this season, looked ordinary.

And on social media, the fallout was immediate.

Aston Martin: Springfield’s Reality Check

Sarah Springfield didn’t mince words. “P19 this weekend, definitely a reality check,” Springfield posted. “Safe to say I don’t like Bahrain.”

A trackside engineer from the team, speaking privately, admitted the car struggled with rear grip throughout the weekend. “The upgrades helped the front end, but the balance was off. Sarah couldn’t push. It’s fixable, but it stings.”

Audi: Keisner Seething

Robin Keisner endured a weekend that unravelled in the pit lane. A second consecutive race with a pit‑stop issue, followed by a collision with Maikel Zwart that saw Keisner — not Zwart — receive a penalty. His tweet read like a man trying to scrub the race from memory.

“Honestly what was this, I’m speechless,” Keisner wrote. “The second pitstop issue in a race, then Zwart diveboming me while I’m ahead of the corner and I get penalized. I want to forget this race as soon as I can. This weekend was just a catastrophe.”

The stewards’ decision to punish Keisner rather than Zwart is already a talking point. Paddock observers noted that the replay appeared to show Zwart lunging from too far back.

An Audi pit‑crew member who worked on Keisner’s car did not hide their disappointment. “We’ve had issues two races in a row now. The pit‑stop problem cost us time, and then the penalty was the final straw. Robin is right to be angry. We need to be better — a lot better.”

Williams: “Mic. Drop.”

Jack Tef was floating. The Williams team principal, standing in for the injured Lucas Koopman, drove from 17th on the grid to a podium finish — third place, right behind his teammate Eren Aygen. Williams’ 2‑3 finish was their best of the season, and it vaulted them to the top of the constructors’ championship.

Tef’s post‑race message on Twitter was pure adrenaline:

“THATS HOW WE DO IT! 2‑3 in Bahrain! This is what the hard work has brought, Grove has reaped what we sow with these upgrades. This is how Williams do it, no media drama, no secret meetings, just focus. Walking into Saudi leading the WCC, and hoping to continue the momentum. Mic. Drop.”

No media drama? In a weekend where Mercedes’ internal tensions still simmered, and Ferrari’s driver swap remained the paddock’s obsession, Tef’s claim was either aspirational or deliberately pointed. Either way, Williams lead the championship. That is not a sentence anyone predicted in March.

Haas: A Weekend to Forget

Team principal Jack Stone offered a sober assessment. “Overall, it’s been a disappointing weekend for us here in Bahrain. We faced a tough start with both cars struggling in qualifying, eliminated in Q1 and Q2 due to traffic issues, which put us on the back foot from the beginning. In the race, George’s unfortunate DNF just before the final corner was a real blow. It’s frustrating to see the car break down at such a critical moment. Afonso managed to finish P17, but clearly, we have a lot of work to do to improve our performance and reliability. We’ll analyze everything thoroughly and come back stronger for the next race.”

Cachinho’s return to Haas was always going to be a quiet affair. But P17, while his temporary replacement Riley Swansson scored points for Ferrari in P9, only sharpens the contrast. The swap may be temporary, but the narratives are hardening.

Ferrari: The Alarm Bell

Eetu Väisänen, the championship leader, did not mince words either. The Finn, who had started last after a shock Q1 exit, recovered to finish 16th — his worst result of the season.

“The parts upgrades everyone brought were huge,” Väisänen said. “We can’t fall back like this again.”

Ferrari brought no upgrades to Bahrain. That decision, made before the team knew the scale of the step others would take, now looks costly. One senior Ferrari engineer, who asked not to be named, admitted the team had been caught out. “We thought our baseline was enough for one more weekend. Clearly it wasn’t. We’ll have new parts in Jeddah. They have to be a step.”

Red Bull: A Missed Opportunity

A mechanic who declined to be named admitted that Roy’s fourth place felt like a missed opportunity. “The car was fast enough for a podium. Jason drove brilliantly, but we got the strategy wrong in the middle stint. We pitted him into traffic, and he had to use the tyres up fighting through. By the time he was in clear air, the gap to the leaders was too big. It’s frustrating.”

Racing Bulls: Unease Creeping In

A senior engineer confessed to a growing sense of unease. “We expected more. Quarter was quick early on, but the mediums fell off a cliff. Lukus’s race was ruined by the pit‑stop delay. We’re looking at a lot of data tonight. Something isn’t adding up.” The team that dominated 2024 and 2025 now sits sixth in the constructors’. The fall has been steep.

Alpine: Bittersweet

A trackside performance analyst described the race as “bittersweet.” MaraDöner’s fifth place was a solid haul, but the intra‑team battle with Julian Kondra earlier in the race had been tense. “Team orders were used to let Michiil past, but the radio exchange wasn’t exactly smooth. There’s some friction there. We’ll manage it, but it’s something to watch.”

McLaren: Jubilation

The mechanics were jubilant. “Joshua deserved that so much,” one said. “After Japan, half the paddock had written him off. But he walked into the garage this morning like nothing had happened. Calm. Focused. The car wasn’t even the quickest today — he just didn’t make a single mistake. That’s what wins races.”

Mercedes: A Sombre Reflection

An engineer, speaking on condition of anonymity, offered a more sombre reflection. “Wesley did a good job for eighth, but Maikel’s race was a mess again. The collision with Keisner — we’re looking at the data, but it’s part of a pattern. The car isn’t where we need it, and the frustration is bleeding into the driving. Upgrades helped, but not enough.”

The Upgrade Table

Every team except Ferrari arrived in Bahrain with new parts. Here is what each team brought:

  • Racing Bulls: Chassis, Sidepods, Suspension
  • Red Bull: Chassis, Underfloor
  • Audi: Front Wing, Sidepods
  • Mercedes: Sidepods, Underfloor
  • Alpine: Chassis, Sidepods, Suspension
  • Aston Martin: Sidepods, Underfloor, Suspension
  • McLaren: Chassis, Underfloor, Suspension
  • Williams: Chassis, Sidepods, Suspension
  • Haas: Chassis, Rear Wing, Underfloor
  • Ferrari: No upgrades

Jeddah waits. The high‑speed Corniche circuit will offer another test — of straight‑line speed, of nerve, and of a Ferrari team that suddenly looks mortal. The Williams party will continue for a few days yet. But in this paddock, euphoria is always temporary. The next race is already looming.

Watch this space.
